topic: DeepEval Tutorial 2026: Pytest-Native LLM Evaluation for Production AI

key_points: DeepEval has emerged as the leading open-source framework for LLM evaluation in 2026
50+ built-in metrics including G-Eval, faithfulness, hallucination detection, answer relevancy, and task completion
Native CI/CD integration via Pytest — best for code-first engineering teams
Covers RAG, agents, chatbots, safety testing — broader than RAGAS
Step-by-step practical guide to using DeepEval with Python
LLMTestCase structure: input, actual_output, expected_output, retrieval_context
Running deepeval test run from CLI for end-to-end evaluation
Integration with Confident AI platform for team dashboards
Practical walkthrough including hallucination detection with HallucinationMetric
G-Eval for custom criteria using chain-of-thought decomposition
Side-by-side code examples for different metric types
FaithfulnessMetric for RAG grounding verification
Beginner-friendly introduction to DeepEval for Python developers
@pytest.mark.parametrize for looping through EvaluationDataset
assert_test() as the core testing primitive
GEval and AnswerRelevancyMetric examples with code
2026 LLM evaluation landscape overview: evaluation woven into CI/CD, not just final QA
DeepEval's pytest-native model differentiates it from notebook-centric tools
Production referenceless metrics: no labeled data required for runtime evals
Async evaluation patterns for non-blocking production monitoring

stats.fact: DeepEval has 15,291 GitHub stars and 1,419 forks as of May 2026, with active daily releases
DeepEval is used by 150,000+ developers with over 100 million daily evaluations
DeepEval is adopted by more than 50% of Fortune 500 companies for LLM quality assurance
DeepEval ships 50+ research-backed, ready-to-use metrics covering RAG, agents, safety, multimodal, and conversational use cases
DeepEval recommends no more than 5 metrics per evaluation: 2-3 generic system-specific + 1-2 custom use-case metrics for production
DeepEval has 250+ open-source contributors and is licensed under Apache 2.0
G-Eval uses LLM-as-a-Judge with chain-of-thought (CoT) to evaluate outputs against ANY natural language criteria without hand-crafted rubrics
DeepEval's StepEfficiencyMetric is critical for production agents, penalizing redundant tool calls and unnecessary reasoning loops that inflate token costs

angles: Pytest-native design lowers the learning curve — Python developers can write LLM evals exactly like unit tests, using familiar assert_test() and @pytest.mark.parametrize patterns
G-Eval as a flexible LLM-as-a-Judge layer: define evaluation criteria in plain English, and CoT decomposition handles the scoring rubric automatically

RAG evaluation completeness: five dedicated metrics (answer relevancy, faithfulness, contextual precision, contextual recall, contextual relevancy) catch different failure modes in retrieval-augmented pipelines
CI/CD gate pattern: how to fail GitHub Actions builds when LLM quality drops below a threshold, treating eval regressions like code regressions
Production async evaluation: referenceless metrics that run without ground-truth labels, non-blocking so they don't slow user responses
Agent evaluation frontier: StepEfficiencyMetric and tool-correctness metrics designed for multi-step agentic systems — the fastest-growing segment of 2026 LLM apps

DeepEval vs RAGAS vs TruLens vs Braintrust — clear decision framework: use DeepEval when evals live in code, RAGAS for pure RAG depth, TruLens for observability integration, Braintrust for product/PM dashboards
outline: H2: What Is DeepEval and Why Pytest-Native LLM Evaluation Matters in 2026
H2: Installing DeepEval and Configuring Your Evaluation Environment
H2: Core Concepts: LLMTestCase, EvaluationDataset, and Metrics
H2: Built-in Metrics Deep Dive — RAG, Hallucination, and Answer Relevancy
H2: G-Eval: Flexible LLM-as-a-Judge Scoring with Custom Criteria
H2: Agent Evaluation — StepEfficiency, Tool Correctness, and Task Completion
H2: Integrating DeepEval into CI/CD Pipelines with GitHub Actions
H2: Production Evaluation Patterns — Async, Referenceless, and Threshold-Based
H2: DeepEval vs RAGAS vs TruLens vs Braintrust — When to Use Each
